sources.citation: Supreme Court of Kenya, Raila Odinga v. Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission [2017]; V-Dem Kenya 2017; EU EOM 2017

2018.assessment:
Post-election stabilization. Kenyatta-Odinga 'Building Bridges Initiative' (BBI)
handshake aimed to reduce ethnic political polarization. Institutions functioning
with continued flaws in anti-corruption and bureaucratic capacity.

sources.citation: V-Dem Kenya 2018; Freedom House 2018

2019.assessment:
Institutional functioning continued with improvement. BBI process ongoing. Anti-corruption
efforts (DPP Noordin Haji, DCI George Kinoti) showed some institutional independence.
Judiciary maintaining independence established in 2010 constitution.

sources.citation: V-Dem Kenya 2019; Freedom House 2019

2020.assessment:
Institutional functioning maintained. BBI constitutional amendment process controversial.
COVID-19 emergency measures raised some institutional concerns. Judiciary continued
to demonstrate independence.

sources.citation: V-Dem Kenya 2020; Freedom House 2020

2021.assessment:
BBI ruled unconstitutional by Court of Appeal (affirmed by Supreme Court 2022) —
another demonstration of judicial independence. Pre-2022 election preparations ongoing.
Institutions functioning with continued flaws.

sources.citation: V-Dem Kenya 2021; Court of Appeal BBI ruling May 2021

2022.assessment:
August 2022 election held. Ruto won presidential election; Odinga challenged in
Supreme Court which upheld results (September 2022) in a credible ruling. Third
peaceful transfer of power. IEBC demonstrated reasonable impartiality. Institutions
functioning well under 2010 constitutional framework.

sources.citation: Supreme Court Kenya, Raila Odinga v. IEBC 2022; V-Dem Kenya 2022; AU-EU EOM 2022

2023.assessment:
Institutional functioning continued under Ruto government. Finance bill political
tensions but parliament, courts, and executive functioning. Anti-corruption
institutions active. Functional but flawed.

sources.citation: V-Dem Kenya 2023; Freedom House 2023

2024.assessment:
Finance bill protests (June 2024) and storming of parliament tested institutions.
Ruto responded by forming a broad-based government including opposition figures.
However, security forces' lethal response to protests (39 deaths) raised concerns
about accountability. Courts issued rulings on the finance bill. Institutions
functional but under stress.

sources.citation: V-Dem Kenya 2024; KNCHR Finance Bill Protest Report 2024; Freedom House 2024

2025.assessment:
Institutional functioning continued under Ruto's broad-based government.
Kenya's constitutional institutions remain among the most developed in East Africa.
Continued challenges from corruption and political manipulation.

sources.citation: V-Dem Kenya 2025 estimate; Freedom House 2025

1995.assessment:
Moderate level of political violence in 1995 under the Moi regime. Ethnic clashes
in the Rift Valley (continuing from 1992-94 violence) caused deaths; state repression
of opposition figures continued. Estimated deaths approximately 100-200. No organized
armed conflict by UCDP thresholds.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset Kenya; Human Rights Watch Kenya 1995

1996.assessment:
Political violence at moderate-low levels. Rift Valley tensions continued but
at lower intensity than 1992-94 peak. Some political repression of opposition.
Estimated deaths approximately 80-120.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 1996; Human Rights Watch Kenya 1996

1997.assessment:
Political violence elevated ahead of December 1997 elections. Moi government
orchestrated ethnic violence in Rift Valley and coastal areas to suppress
opposition support. 'Kaya Bomber' attacks in Coast province. Approximately
200-250 deaths from politically-motivated violence.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, 'Playing with Fire: Weapons Proliferation, Political Violence, and Human Rights in Kenya' 2002; UCDP GED 1997

1998.assessment:
The August 7, 1998 US Embassy bombing in Nairobi by Al-Qaeda killed 213 people
(mostly Kenyan) and injured over 4,000. This was a massive single-event terrorist
attack. Background political violence remained at low levels. Total deaths including
the bombing attack approximately 250-350.

sources.citation: US State Department; UCDP GED Kenya 1998; Human Rights Watch

1999.assessment:
Political violence at low-moderate levels. Moi government's repression of
political opponents continued. Ethnic tensions in Rift Valley persisted but
at reduced levels from 1997 peak.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 1999; Human Rights Watch Kenya 1999

2000.assessment:
Political violence at low levels. Moi approaching final term limit; some
political jockeying for succession but not at violent levels.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2000; US State Department Human Rights Report 2000

2001.assessment:
Political violence at low levels. NARC opposition coalition forming; political
tensions existed but without major violent incidents.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2001

2002.assessment:
Political violence at low levels. December 2002 election was largely peaceful —
a major historic achievement. The Mombasa Paradise Hotel bombing (November, 13
deaths) by Al-Qaeda-affiliated group was the main violent incident. Overall
political violence at minimal levels given peaceful transition.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2002; Election Observation Mission report (December 2002)

2003.assessment:
Low political violence. Kibaki government's first year. Post-election euphoria
helped dampen tensions. Some ethnic clashes in Rift Valley continued at low levels.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2003; Human Rights Watch Kenya 2003

2004.assessment:
Low political violence. Some ethnic clashes in northern Kenya (Turkana-Pokot)
and Rift Valley. Kibaki government NARC coalition fracturing did not produce
significant violence.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2004

2005.assessment:
Low political violence. Constitutional referendum (November 2005) — Kibaki's
draft rejected — was politically significant but largely peaceful.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2005; AU-EU referendum observation report

2006.assessment:
Low political violence. Mount Elgon insurgency (Sabaot Land Defence Force — SLDF)
began in 2006 as a land-rights based armed group in western Kenya, causing some deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2006; Human Rights Watch, 'All the Men Have Gone'

2007.assessment:
Post-election violence erupted following the disputed December 27, 2007 election
results. Although most deaths occurred in January-February 2008, December 2007
saw the beginning of systematic political violence with approximately 200-400 deaths
in December alone. The CIPEV (Waki Commission) documented 1,133 deaths in total
for the post-election violence period (December 2007 – February 2008).

sources.citation: CIPEV (Waki Commission) Report, October 2008; UCDP GED Kenya 2007

2008.assessment:
Post-election violence of January-February 2008 killed approximately 1,133-1,200
people (CIPEV/Waki figure) and displaced approximately 600,000. Violence was
politically orchestrated along ethnic lines (Kikuyu vs. Luo, Kalenjin, others).
The Annan-brokered National Accord (February 28, 2008) ended the acute violence.
Mount Elgon SLDF insurgency also continued, causing additional deaths. This was
Kenya's worst episode of political violence since independence.

sources.citation: CIPEV (Waki) Report 2008; Human Rights Watch 'Ballots to Bullets' March 2008; UCDP GED 2008

2009.assessment:
Political violence dropped dramatically from 2008 peak following National Accord.
Mount Elgon SLDF insurgency continued; Kenya Army crackdown (Operation Okoa Maisha)
also produced deaths through extrajudicial killing. Total approximately 200-300 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2009; Human Rights Watch, 'All the Men Have Gone', 2009

2010.assessment:
Political violence at moderate-low levels. SLDF insurgency largely suppressed.
New constitution referendum campaign was peaceful despite ethnic tensions.
Some ethnic clashes in Rift Valley. Approximately 150-200 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2010

2011.assessment:
Political violence increased significantly in 2011 following Kenya's deployment
to Somalia. Al-Shabaab retaliatory attacks in northeastern Kenya began. Ethnic
violence in Tana River Delta (coast) also caused significant casualties.
Approximately 300-400 deaths from combined sources.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2011; ACLED Kenya 2011

2012.assessment:
Al-Shabaab attacks in northeastern Kenya continued. Tana River inter-ethnic
violence (August-September 2012) killed over 100 people. Approximately 350-450
deaths from political violence including Al-Shabaab attacks and ethnic clashes.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2012; ACLED Kenya 2012; UNHCR Tana River situation

2013.assessment:
Westgate Shopping Mall attack (September 2013, 67 deaths) was the major single
event. Combined with ongoing Al-Shabaab attacks in northeastern Kenya and some
election-related violence, total deaths approximately 350-450.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2013; ACLED Kenya 2013; Westgate attack reporting

2014.assessment:
Mpeketoni massacre (June 2014, ~60 deaths) was a major single event. Al-Shabaab
attacks in Lamu, Mandera, Wajir counties continued at high intensity. Total
deaths approximately 400-500 from political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2014; ACLED Kenya 2014

2015.assessment:
Garissa University College attack (April 2, 2015) killed 147 students — Kenya's
deadliest domestic attack. Combined with continued Al-Shabaab attacks throughout
northeastern Kenya and Nairobi, total deaths approximately 550-700. This was
the peak year for Al-Shabaab-related violence in Kenya.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2015; ACLED Kenya 2015; Garissa attack reporting

2016.assessment:
Political violence declined from 2015 peak. Al-Shabaab attacks continued but
at lower frequency and scale. Approximately 300-400 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2016; ACLED Kenya 2016

2017.assessment:
Election-related violence following the disputed August 2017 election and the
annulled result. Security forces killed approximately 37 opposition supporters
in post-election protests (Amnesty International). Al-Shabaab attacks continued.
Total approximately 300-400 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2017; ACLED Kenya 2017; Amnesty International, 'Halt Unlawful Killings' 2017

2018.assessment:
Political violence declined from 2017. Al-Shabaab attacks continued in northeastern
Kenya at lower scale. 'Handshake' political settlement between Kenyatta and Odinga
reduced elite conflict. Approximately 200 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2018; ACLED Kenya 2018

2019.assessment:
Dusit D2 Hotel attack (January 2019, 21 deaths). Al-Shabaab attacks continued
in northeastern Kenya. Overall approximately 200 deaths from political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2019; ACLED Kenya 2019

2020.assessment:
Al-Shabaab attacks continued in northeastern Kenya. Lamu Maritime airbase attack
(January 2020) killed 3 Americans. Police violence during COVID-19 curfew enforcement
killed approximately 15 people (IPOA data). Total approximately 200 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2020; ACLED Kenya 2020; IPOA Covid enforcement report

2021.assessment:
Continued Al-Shabaab attacks in northeastern Kenya. Ethnic clashes in Rift Valley
continued at low levels. Approximately 200 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2021; ACLED Kenya 2021

2022.assessment:
August 2022 election largely peaceful despite earlier concerns. Some post-election
protests but security forces largely restrained. Al-Shabaab attacks continued.
Approximately 200 deaths.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2022; ACLED Kenya 2022; election observation reports 2022

2023.assessment:
Continued Al-Shabaab attacks in northeastern Kenya. Ethnic clashes in some counties.
Approximately 200 deaths. Finance bill protests in mid-2023 were peaceful compared to
2024 protests.

sources.citation: UCDP GED Kenya 2023; ACLED Kenya 2023

2024.assessment:
Finance bill protests in June 2024 resulted in deaths — protesters stormed parliament
on June 25, 2024 and security forces killed approximately 39 protesters (Kenya National
Commission on Human Rights figure), making it the deadliest domestic political violence
since 2017. Combined with continued Al-Shabaab attacks in northeastern Kenya, total
approximately 250-300 deaths.

sources.citation: Kenya National Commission on Human Rights, Finance Bill Protest Report 2024; ACLED Kenya 2024

2025.assessment:
Al-Shabaab attacks continuing in northeastern Kenya at lower frequency. Political
tensions remain elevated post-2024 protests but no major episodes of mass violence.
Estimated deaths approximately 150-200.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2025 estimate

1995.assessment:
The Kenyan government under President Moi exercised effective central control over
the national territory. No significant insurgencies or separatist movements challenged
state authority. Some banditry and cattle raiding in arid northern and northeastern
regions (Turkana, Mandera) were chronic low-level security issues but did not
constitute territorial challenges.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 1995; IISS Military Balance 1995

1996.assessment:
Full government territorial control maintained. Continued low-level banditry in
northern regions but no organized insurgency challenging state authority.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 1996

1997.assessment:
Full government territorial control maintained. Ethnic clashes in Rift Valley
and coastal areas in 1997 were politically orchestrated but localized; they
did not represent challenges to national territorial authority.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Kenya: Politically Motivated Ethnic Violence, 1997

1998.assessment:
Full government territorial control. Al-Qaeda bombed the US Embassy in Nairobi
in August 1998 (224 deaths), a major terrorist attack, but this was a single
incident by a foreign network, not a territorial challenge to Kenyan state authority.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 1998; US State Department

1999.assessment: Full government territorial control maintained.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 1999

2000.assessment: Full government territorial control maintained.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 2000

2001.assessment: Full government territorial control maintained.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 2001

2002.assessment:
Full government territorial control maintained. Mombasa Paradise Hotel bombing
(November 2002, Al-Qaeda affiliated, 13 deaths) was a terrorist attack but not
a territorial challenge. Peaceful electoral transition did not affect territorial control.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 2002

2003.assessment: Full government territorial control maintained under new Kibaki government.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 2003

2004.assessment: Full government territorial control maintained.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 2004

2005.assessment: Full government territorial control maintained.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 2005

2006.assessment: Full government territorial control maintained.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 2006

2007.assessment:
Full government territorial control maintained through election year. Post-election
violence beginning December 2007 was not a territorial insurgency but rather
politically orchestrated ethnic violence; the state retained overall authority
though temporarily unable to enforce security in some flashpoint areas.

sources.citation: CIPEV (Waki Commission) Report 2008; Human Rights Watch Kenya 2008

2008.assessment:
The post-election violence of January-February 2008 saw significant breakdown of
state authority in parts of Rift Valley, Nairobi slums (Kibera, Mathare), and
western Kenya. Government security forces struggled to contain violence; approximately
1,200 deaths and 600,000 displaced. However, the government never lost overall
territorial authority and the violence subsided after the Annan-mediated National
Accord. By February 28, 2008 the power-sharing agreement restored stability.

sources.citation: CIPEV Waki Commission Report 2008; Human Rights Watch, 'Ballots to Bullets', March 2008

2009.assessment:
Full government territorial control restored following the National Accord
power-sharing settlement. Peace largely maintained though tensions remained.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 2009

2010.assessment:
Full government territorial control. New constitution passed by referendum (August 2010)
enhanced institutional framework. No significant territorial challenges.

sources.citation: CIA World Factbook Kenya 2010

2011.assessment:
Kenya's territorial control increasingly challenged by Al-Shabaab as Kenya deployed
forces to Somalia in October 2011 (Operation Linda Nchi). Al-Shabaab began
retaliatory attacks in northeastern Kenya and Nairobi. The northeastern region
(Mandera, Wajir, Garissa) saw heightened insecurity, but government retained
overall authority there.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya data 2011; Human Rights Watch, Kenya-Somalia cross-border operations

2012.assessment:
Continued Al-Shabaab threats in northeastern Kenya and coastal areas. Kidnappings
of tourists and aid workers; grenade attacks in Nairobi. Government retained
territorial authority but insurgent activity continued.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2012; UCDP Armed Conflict Dataset

2013.assessment:
Al-Shabaab attacks continued. The September 2013 Westgate Shopping Mall attack
(Nairobi, 67 deaths) was a major atrocity. Kenya retained full territorial
authority but faced ongoing Al-Shabaab insurgency in northeastern and coastal areas.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2013; UCDP; Westgate attack reporting (September 2013)

2014.assessment:
Al-Shabaab attacks intensified in northeastern and coastal regions. Mpeketoni
massacre (June 2014, ~60 deaths) was particularly severe. Government deployed
additional security forces but insurgent activity remained significant.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2014; Amnesty International; Kenya Police Service reports

2015.assessment:
April 2015 Garissa University attack (147 deaths) was the deadliest Al-Shabaab
attack in Kenya. Northeastern Kenya (Garissa, Mandera, Wajir) remained persistently
insecure with government control contested in rural areas. Overall territorial
authority maintained though northeastern region had reduced effective control.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2015; UN OCHA Kenya; ICG East Africa Briefing 2015

2016.assessment:
Continued Al-Shabaab attacks in northeastern Kenya though frequency declined from 2015
peak. Government security operations (GSU, KDF) maintained presence in Mandera
and Garissa counties. Coastal area also saw some insecurity.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2016; KDF press statements

2017.assessment:
Al-Shabaab attacks continued in northeastern Kenya but at reduced frequency from
2015 peak. Electoral tensions and post-election protests did not affect territorial
control. Overall government authority maintained.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2017

2018.assessment:
Al-Shabaab attacks continued at lower frequency in northeastern Kenya. Government
maintained effective security operations in the region. Dusit D2 Hotel attack
in January 2019 (21 deaths) would come later; 2018 relatively quieter.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2018

2019.assessment:
January 2019 Dusit D2 Hotel attack in Nairobi (21 deaths) showed Al-Shabaab's
reach into the capital. Northeastern Kenya continued to face insurgent activity.
Overall territorial control maintained but northeastern region remained insecure.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2019; Dusit attack reporting January 2019

2020.assessment:
Al-Shabaab attacks continued in northeastern Kenya. Coronavirus pandemic limited
some population movement. Lamu Maritime attacks in January 2020 (US airbase).
Overall territorial control maintained by government forces.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2020

2021.assessment:
Continued Al-Shabaab activity in northeastern Kenya. Government maintained security
deployments. Overall effective territorial authority maintained.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2021

2022.assessment:
Continued Al-Shabaab activity in northeastern Kenya. Ruto's election did not
affect territorial control. Government maintained security presence in affected
counties.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2022

2023.assessment:
Continued Al-Shabaab activity in northeastern Kenya. Ruto government increased
security operations in the region. Protests against finance bill had no territorial dimension.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2023

2024.assessment:
Al-Shabaab attacks continued in northeastern Kenya. Finance bill protests (June 2024)
were nationwide demonstrations not territorial challenges. Kenya deployed troops to Haiti
under MMAS (Multinational Security Support) mission. Overall territorial authority maintained.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2024; Kenya National Police Service

2025.assessment:
Continued low-level Al-Shabaab activity in northeastern Kenya. Government maintained
effective territorial authority. Kenya Kwanza government continued counterinsurgency
operations in border areas.

sources.citation: ACLED Kenya 2025 estimate; KDF press releases

1995.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 1995. OBS did not begin until 2006. Under Moi's
KANU, budget transparency was limited but Kenya published budget estimates
and annual reports as required by Commonwealth parliamentary practice.
Budget documents were available to parliament but public access was limited.

sources.citation: Kenya Government Budget Estimates, 1995; IMF Article IV Kenya 1995

1996.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 1996. Kenya maintained basic parliamentary budget
procedures. Annual budget presented to National Assembly. Some donor
transparency requirements through IMF/World Bank engagement. Limited public access.

sources.citation: Kenya National Assembly records, 1996; IMF Kenya Article IV

1997.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 1997. Budget presented to parliament. IMF
conditionality holds (governance concerns) meant some fiscal data
was produced for international audiences but not always public.
Overall budget transparency limited under KANU.

sources.citation: IMF Kenya Article IV Consultation, 1997

1998.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 1998. Budget processes functional under parliamentary
system. Some IMF engagement improving data quality. Public budget access
remained limited under Moi administration.

sources.citation: IMF Kenya Article IV, 1998

1999.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 1999. Budget presented to National Assembly.
Late Moi era, budget transparency modest but parliamentary process maintained.
IMF data requirements partially met. Public budget documents limited.

sources.citation: IMF Kenya Article IV, 1999

2000.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 2000. Budget transparency remained modest
under Moi's final years. Parliamentary budget process continued.
Limited public access to detailed budget documents. Some external
accountability through PRSP process (IMF/WB requirements).

sources.citation: Kenya PRSP process 2000; IMF Kenya Article IV

2001.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 2001. Budget transparency limited but parliamentary
process functioning. Pre-transition period; Kibaki opposition pressing for
accountability. Some MTEF (medium-term expenditure framework) reforms begun.

sources.citation: Kenya Ministry of Finance, MTEF introduction records, 2001

2002.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 2002. Kibaki election in December promised
improved governance and transparency. NARC coalition campaigned on
anti-corruption and transparency platform. Pre-OBS; budget transparency
modest under transitioning KANU.

sources.citation: Kenya National Assembly Budget records, 2002

2003.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 2003. Kibaki government began governance reforms
including budget transparency improvements. MTEF strengthened. Budget
documents becoming more publicly available. Moderate improvement from Moi era.

sources.citation: Kenya Ministry of Finance reforms, 2003; IMF Kenya Article IV 2003

2004.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 2004. Budget transparency improving under Kibaki.
Medium-term budget framework published. Anti-corruption stance initially
strengthening transparency. Anglo Leasing scandal somewhat undermining
overall governance transparency.

sources.citation: Kenya National Assembly budget records 2004; IMF Kenya 2004

2005.assessment:
No OBS data for Kenya in 2005. Budget transparency at moderate levels.
Kenya's parliamentary budget process functioning with some public access.
IMF PRGF program requirements improving fiscal data. Corruption scandals
dampening overall governance progress.

sources.citation: IMF Kenya PRGF Program review, 2005

2006.assessment:
Kenya's OBS score was 49 in the inaugural 2006 survey. This ranked Kenya
in the moderate category, one of the better performers in Sub-Saharan Africa.
Kenya published a budget proposal, enacted budget, and in-year reports.
Year-end reports and audit reports had limited public availability.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2006, Kenya

2007.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2007. Interpolated around 2006 (OBI=49) and
2008 (OBI=57). Budget transparency moderately good and improving.
Pre-election period; budget process functioning.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey interpolated between 2006 and 2008

2008.assessment:
Kenya's OBS score improved to 57 in 2008, ranking it as providing
'significant' budget information to citizens. Kenya was among the
stronger performers in Africa despite post-election violence disruption.
Grand Coalition continued budget transparency commitments.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008, Kenya

2009.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2009. Interpolated between 2008 (OBI=57) and
2010 (OBI=48). Grand Coalition governance maintained budget transparency
at moderate to significant levels.

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2008-2010

2010.assessment:
Kenya's OBS score declined to 48 in 2010. New constitution (August 2010)
embedded public finance management principles but full implementation
pending. Some budget documents less publicly available than 2008.
Still one of Africa's better performers.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010, Kenya

2011.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2011. New constitution PFM provisions beginning
to be implemented. Budget transparency at moderate levels, interpolated
around 2010 (OBI=48) and 2012 (OBI=49).

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2010-2012

2012.assessment:
Kenya's OBS score was 49 in 2012. Moderate budget transparency maintained.
County devolution under new constitution beginning to require sub-national
budget transparency. National budget process improving in accessibility.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012, Kenya

2013.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2013. Devolved governance (counties became
operational March 2013) expanded budget transparency requirements.
National budget improving; county budgets new and less transparent initially.
Interpolated around 2012 (OBI=49) and 2015 (OBI=46).

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2012-2015; Kenya PFM Act 2012

2014.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2014. Budget transparency at moderate levels.
County systems improving. National budget process well-established.
Interpolated between 2012 (OBI=49) and 2015 (OBI=46).

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2012-2015

2015.assessment:
Kenya's OBS score was 46 in 2015. Modest decline from 2012. Kenya
published most key budget documents but citizen budget and some audit
reports were not consistently published. Moderate transparency overall.
County budgets improving but national score slightly declined.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2015, Kenya

2016.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2016. Budget transparency at moderate levels,
interpolated between 2015 (OBI=46) and 2017 (OBI=46). Kenya Budget
Statement and Estimates readily available online. Moderate transparency.

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2015-2017

2017.assessment:
Kenya's OBS score was 46 in 2017. Stable moderate transparency despite
election controversy. Budget documents publicly available. Kenya one of
Africa's stronger performers. Pre-budget statement and audit reports
had some gaps.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2017, Kenya

2018.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2018. Budget transparency at moderate levels.
Interpolated around 2017 (OBI=46) and 2019 (OBI=45). Kenya maintained
budget publication practices. Devolved system improving county transparency.

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2017-2019

2019.assessment:
Kenya's OBS score was 45 in 2019. Slightly below 2017. Budget documents
publicly available but some gaps in audit report publication and citizen
budget. Kenya above Sub-Saharan African average. Moderate transparency.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019, Kenya

2020.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2020. Budget transparency affected by COVID-19
emergency spending which reduced some normal transparency requirements.
Supplementary budgets issued. Overall moderate transparency maintained.
Interpolated around 2019 (OBI=45) and 2021 (OBI=48).

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2019-2021

2021.assessment:
Kenya's OBS score improved to 48 in 2021. Modest improvement post-COVID.
Budget documents more consistently published. Still below the 61 threshold
for 'significant' transparency. Above-average for Sub-Saharan Africa.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2021, Kenya

2022.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2022. Budget transparency at moderate levels.
Ruto government elected August 2022, bringing new fiscal agenda.
Interpolated around 2021 (OBI=48) and 2023 (OBI=53 estimated).
New government committed to fiscal transparency.

sources.citation: IBP OBS interpolated 2021-2023

2023.assessment:
Kenya's OBS score improved to 53 in 2023. Ruto government's Finance
Bill controversy led to greater scrutiny of budget processes. Budget
documents more consistently published. Pre-budget statement improvement.
Moderate to significant transparency level.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2023, Kenya

2024.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2024. Finance Bill protests (June 2024) led to
greater public engagement with budget issues and some transparency
improvements. Budget documents available online. Moderate transparency
approximately similar to 2023 level.

sources.citation: Kenya National Treasury, Budget 2024/25

2025.assessment:
No OBS survey year for 2025. Kenya expected to maintain moderate budget
transparency (approximately OBI 50-55 based on recent trend). Budget
documents available online. Public finance management reforms continuing
under Ruto government.

sources.citation: Kenya National Treasury overview 2025

1995.assessment:
Kenya has no significant oil, gas, or mining extractive sector to speak of
in 1995. Kenya's economy is based on agriculture, tourism, and services.
No EITI relevance. Extractive transparency not applicable in the traditional
resource-curse sense. No major extractive revenues to disclose.

sources.citation: World Bank Kenya country overview, extractive sector, 1995

1996.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 1996. EITI not applicable.
Some small-scale gold and soda ash mining existed but not significant
revenue source requiring EITI-level transparency.

sources.citation: Kenya Mining Act records, 1996

1997.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 1997. Some soda ash
extraction at Lake Magadi. No oil or gas. EITI not applicable.

sources.citation: Kenya extractive sector overview 1997

1998.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 1998. EITI not applicable.
Kenya economy dominated by agriculture, tea, horticulture, tourism.
No oil, gas, or significant mining revenues.

sources.citation: World Bank Kenya country profile, 1998

1999.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 1999. EITI not applicable.
Oil exploration ongoing in northern Kenya but no commercial discoveries.
Extractive transparency not a governance priority.

sources.citation: Kenya Ministry of Petroleum records, 1999

2000.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2000. Continued oil
exploration without commercial success. Soda ash at Magadi only
notable mineral production. EITI not applicable.

sources.citation: Kenya extractive sector overview 2000

2001.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2001. EITI was founded
in 2003 and Kenya was not among initial candidate countries.
No extractive revenues requiring EITI disclosure.

sources.citation: EITI founding records 2003; Kenya extractive sector

2002.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2002. EITI not applicable.
Regime change brought new governance agenda but extractive sector
too small to require EITI-level transparency.

sources.citation: Kenya extractive sector overview 2002

2003.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2003. EITI founded in 2003
(Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative). Kenya not a candidate.
No oil/gas commercial production. Mining sector small.

sources.citation: EITI founding declaration 2003; Kenya sector assessment

2004.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2004. Kenya not an EITI
candidate country. Oil exploration by Tullow Oil and others ongoing
in Turkana with no commercial success yet.

sources.citation: EITI candidate countries list 2004; Kenya petroleum records

2005.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2005. EITI not applicable.
Oil exploration ongoing without commercial result. Small gold mining
in western Kenya. Soda ash extraction. No significant extractive revenues.

sources.citation: EITI records 2005; Kenya extractive overview

2006.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2006. EITI not applicable.
Continued oil exploration in Turkana Basin. Some geothermal development
(energy sector) but not EITI-relevant extractive production.

sources.citation: EITI records 2006; Kenya extractive overview

2007.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2007. EITI not applicable.
Vision 2030 flagged extractive sector development as a future goal
but no current commercial production warranting EITI transparency.

sources.citation: Kenya Vision 2030 extractive sector goals; EITI records 2007

2008.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2008. EITI not applicable.
Continued oil and gas exploration. No commercial oil production.
Soda ash and small-scale gold not requiring EITI framework.

sources.citation: EITI records 2008; Kenya petroleum department records

2009.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2009. EITI not applicable.
Turkana Basin exploration continuing. No commercial finds yet.
Kenya's economy still based on agriculture, services, tourism.

sources.citation: EITI records 2009; Kenya extractive sector overview

2010.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2010. EITI not applicable.
New constitution (2010) included natural resources provisions requiring
equitable sharing of resource revenues, but no commercial production yet.

sources.citation: Kenya Constitution 2010, natural resources provisions; EITI records

2011.assessment:
No significant extractive sector in Kenya in 2011. Tullow Oil and Africa
Oil Corporation drilling in Turkana increased exploration activity.
No commercial discovery yet. EITI not applicable.

sources.citation: Tullow Oil Kenya operations 2011; EITI records

2012.assessment:
Oil discovered in Turkana County (March 2012). Kenya now classified as an
oil-producing country in potential, but no production yet. Kenya began
considering EITI candidacy. EITI standard not yet adopted.
Extractive sector becoming relevant.

sources.citation: Tullow Oil Kenya oil discovery announcement, March 2012

2013.assessment:
Kenya oil discovery confirmed; EITI candidacy being discussed in government.
Kenya Mining Forum exploring EITI framework. No commercial oil production yet.
Kenya Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative discussions beginning.
Pre-candidate stage.

sources.citation: Kenya Ministry of Energy extractive transparency records, 2013

2014.assessment:
Kenya expressed interest in joining EITI in 2014. Natural Resources
Management legislation moving through parliament. Oil production development
planning (FID expected in 2016-2017). Kenya EITI multi-stakeholder group
forming. Candidate status pending.

sources.citation: EITI Kenya candidacy application records, 2014

2015.assessment:
Kenya became an EITI Candidate Country in 2016 (formal application 2015-2016).
Kenya EITI (KEITI) established. Multi-stakeholder group (MSG) formed.
First KEITI report being prepared. Extractive sector transparency formally
beginning.

sources.citation: EITI Kenya candidate admission 2016; KEITI establishment

2016.assessment:
Kenya formally admitted as EITI Candidate Country in 2016. Kenya EITI
(KEITI) began operating with Multi-Stakeholder Group. First KEITI Annual
Report being developed. Significant progress on extractive transparency
framework.

sources.citation: EITI, Kenya Candidate Country status, 2016

2017.assessment:
Kenya EITI Candidate status ongoing. First KEITI Report published (2016
data). No commercial oil production yet (oil development delayed). Gold,
soda ash, fluorspar extraction reported. Extractive revenues small but disclosed.

sources.citation: KEITI First Report 2017; EITI Kenya progress

2018.assessment:
Kenya EITI Candidate continuing. Second KEITI Report published. Oil development
continuing in Turkana (Early Oil Pilot Scheme 2018 — small volumes exported).
KEITI covering petroleum, mining, and related revenues. Kenya achieving
good progress toward EITI compliance.

sources.citation: KEITI Second Report 2018; Early Oil Pilot Scheme Kenya

2019.assessment:
Kenya EITI Candidate status. KEITI annual reporting continuing. EITI
validation expected. Oil production delayed commercially but transparency
framework in place. Kenya progressing toward compliance.

sources.citation: EITI Kenya validation assessment 2019

2020.assessment:
Kenya EITI Candidate status maintained. EITI validation completed with
some findings. COVID-19 delayed some reporting. Oil development continued
facing commercial challenges. KEITI reporting operational.

sources.citation: EITI Kenya validation report 2020

2021.assessment:
Kenya remained EITI Candidate. KEITI reports continuing. EITI Board
issued corrective action requirements. Kenya making progress toward
compliance. Oil development in Turkana facing ongoing commercial and
infrastructure challenges.

sources.citation: EITI Kenya corrective action record, 2021

2022.assessment:
Kenya remained EITI Candidate. KEITI active with regular reporting.
Kenya progressing on EITI requirements. Oil sector still not commercially
operational at scale. Gold, fluorspar, soda ash covered in KEITI reports.

sources.citation: EITI Kenya status 2022; KEITI annual report

2023.assessment:
Kenya remained EITI Candidate Country. Ruto government supportive of
extractive transparency. KEITI operational. Oil development in Turkana
still facing commercial barriers. Mining sector (gold, fluorspar, coal)
covered under KEITI.

sources.citation: EITI Kenya status 2023; KEITI reports

2024.assessment:
Kenya remains EITI Candidate. KEITI continuing work. Oil development
in Turkana still facing financial and commercial barriers. Extractive
transparency framework operational though sector small relative to economy.

sources.citation: EITI Kenya status 2024

2025.assessment:
Kenya remains an EITI Candidate Country as of early 2025. KEITI operational.
Commercial oil production at scale remains pending. Extractive transparency
framework in place and functioning for existing mining revenues.

sources.citation: EITI Kenya overview 2025

1995.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 1995. WJP Rule of Law Index began in 2012.
Under Moi's KANU, rule of law was limited. Courts nominally independent
but executive interference was documented. Key laws gazetted but enforcement
uneven. Judicial corruption known. Legal transparency limited.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 1995

1996.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 1996. Legal transparency limited under KANU.
Courts operating but subject to executive pressure. Detention without
trial documented. Law Reform Commission existed but limited impact.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 1996

1997.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 1997. Limited legal transparency. IPPG reforms
(before 1997 election) included some judicial provisions. Courts
nominally independent but executive dominance persisted. Constitution
debates ongoing.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 1997

1998.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 1998. Legal transparency limited. Constitution
of Kenya Review Commission established 2000 (anticipating). Existing courts
functional but with executive interference. Limited legal transparency.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 1998

1999.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 1999. Legal transparency remained limited under
Moi. Courts under executive pressure. Constitution Reform Commission
established to develop new constitution. Parliamentary committees limited.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 1999

2000.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2000. Limited legal transparency. Constitution
of Kenya Review Commission (CKRC) active. Courts functional but executive
interference documented. Judicial corruption affecting rule of law.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2000

2001.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2001. Limited legal transparency continuing.
CKRC drafts being developed. Judicial reforms being discussed by opposition
as key accountability issue. Limited rule of law under late Moi.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2001

2002.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2002. Legal transparency limited under outgoing
KANU but Kibaki election (December) promised major judicial reforms.
NARC coalition had anti-corruption and rule of law mandate.
Pre-reform conditions.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2002

2003.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2003. Significant legal transparency improvements
under Kibaki. Mass judicial vetting (Ringera Commission) — 82 judges/magistrates
removed for corruption. Anti-Corruption Commission established. Substantially
improved rule of law momentum. Freedom House upgraded Kenya.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2003; Ringera Commission report

2004.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2004. Legal transparency improved post-judicial
vetting. Courts operating with greater independence. Anti-corruption
efforts ongoing but Anglo Leasing scandal showed rule of law limits.
Moderate but improved legal transparency.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2004

2005.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2005. Legal transparency at moderate levels.
Courts more independent than Moi era. Constitutional referendum showed
legal processes functioning. Corruption undermining rule of law quality.
Freedom House rated Kenya 'Partly Free' transitioning to better.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2005

2006.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2006. Moderate legal transparency. Courts
increasingly independent. Multiple high-profile corruption cases pursued.
Legal Aid scheme beginning. Access to justice expanding. Moderate overall.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2006

2007.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2007. Legal transparency at moderate levels.
Pre-election environment showed some legal system strain. Post-election
violence challenged rule of law. Emergency powers invoked limited
legal transparency in final weeks.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2007

2008.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2008. Legal transparency weakened during
post-election violence. Emergency powers, security force impunity,
and lack of accountability for PEV reduced rule of law quality.
National Accord partially restored legal order.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2008

2009.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2009. Legal transparency recovering post-PEV.
ICC jurisdiction over PEV perpetrators accepted. Judicial system resuming
normal operations. National Accord Grand Coalition restored some legal
institutions. Moderate legal transparency.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom in the World, Kenya 2009

2010.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2010. Legal transparency significantly improved
with new constitution (August 2010). New Bill of Rights, Supreme Court
established, independent judiciary provisions strengthened. Major
constitutional transformation.

sources.citation: Kenya Constitution 2010; Freedom House Kenya 2010

2011.assessment:
No WJP data for Kenya in 2011. Legal transparency improving under new
constitution implementation. Judicial Service Commission restructured.
Chief Justice appointed through merit-based process. Courts gaining
independence. Moderate legal transparency improving.

sources.citation: Judicial Service Commission Kenya records, 2011

2012.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.52 (52/100) in 2012,
the first available year. Moderate legal transparency. New constitution
being implemented. Judiciary gaining independence. Public access to
laws improving. Below-average for open government globally but improving.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2012, Kenya

2013.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.52 in 2013 (interpolated).
Devolved governance operational; county governments expanding access to
services and information. Legal transparency maintained at moderate level.
ICC cases ongoing but courts functioning.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index interpolated 2012-2014, Kenya

2014.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.50 in 2014. Slight
decline from 2012. ICC cases withdrawn; government less open about
accountability. Legal transparency moderate but under some executive pressure.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2014, Kenya

2015.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.50 in 2015. Stable
moderate transparency. Judiciary continues functioning with relative
independence. Access to information improving. Security laws restricting
some open government aspects.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2015, Kenya

2016.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.51 in 2016. Slight
improvement. Access to Information Act adopted 2016, significantly improving
legal framework for government transparency. Implementation challenges remained.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2016, Kenya; Access to Information Act 2016

2017.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.51 in 2017. Stable.
Supreme Court nullifying August 2017 election was landmark judicial
independence demonstration. Positive for rule of law. TV shutdowns
somewhat offset by strong judiciary.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017-18, Kenya

2018.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.51 in 2018. TV shutdown
raised concerns about open government. Handshake reduced political pressure.
Judiciary maintained independence. Moderate rule of law overall.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index interpolated 2017-2019, Kenya

2019.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.50 in 2019. Moderate.
Access to Information Act implementation improving. Courts active.
Corruption cases moving through courts. Moderate legal transparency overall.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019, Kenya

2020.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.49 in 2020. Slight
decline. COVID-19 emergency measures reduced some open government norms.
Emergency regulations bypassed parliamentary process. Moderate but declining.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020, Kenya

2021.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.50 in 2021. Moderate.
Post-COVID recovery of legal norms. Courts active. BBI constitutional
change process went through courts showing legal transparency.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021, Kenya

2022.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.50 in 2022. Stable.
Supreme Court validated Ruto election (September 2022). Courts demonstrating
independence. Moderate legal transparency maintained.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2022, Kenya

2023.assessment:
Kenya's WJP Open Government score approximately 0.50 in 2023. Finance Bill
protests showed active civil society engaging legal processes. Courts
heard challenges to Finance Bill. Moderate rule of law with active civil society.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2023, Kenya

2024.assessment:
No WJP 2024 data yet available. Kenya legal transparency estimated at
moderate levels similar to 2023. Gen Z protests (June 2024) tested
rule of law — courts issued injunctions that were initially respected.
Moderate legal transparency.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index trend 2019-2023, Kenya

2025.assessment:
No WJP 2025 data available. Kenya expected to maintain moderate legal
transparency (~0.49-0.51 WJP score) based on recent trend. Judiciary
active. Access to Information Act operational. Moderate rule of law.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index trend, Kenya

1995.assessment:
No RSF data for Kenya in 1995. Under Moi's KANU, press freedom was
severely restricted. The government controlled KNTV and KBC.
Independent newspapers existed (Daily Nation, Standard) but faced
harassment and periodic censorship. Journalists were detained.
Press freedom categorized as problematic to difficult.

sources.citation: CPJ Committee to Protect Journalists, Kenya 1995

1996.assessment:
No RSF data for Kenya in 1996. Press freedom remained problematic
under Moi. State media dominant. Independent papers (Daily Nation, Standard)
functioning but under pressure. Some journalists arrested.
Pre-election year with increased media pressure.

sources.citation: CPJ, Kenya 1996; Freedom House Freedom of the Press, Kenya 1996

1997.assessment:
No RSF data for Kenya in 1997. Election year saw increased government
pressure on independent media. Some journalists detained. KBC used
as KANU propaganda tool. Daily Nation and Standard maintained some
editorial independence under pressure. Freedom House rated 'Not Free' to 'Partly Free'.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press, Kenya 1997; CPJ Kenya 1997

1998.assessment:
No RSF data for Kenya in 1998. Press freedom problematic under Moi.
Independent media functioning under limitations. Post-election, some
relaxation. US Embassy bombing coverage relatively free.
Overall press conditions difficult but not fully closed.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press, Kenya 1998

1999.assessment:
No RSF data for Kenya in 1999. Press freedom problematic. Late Moi era
maintained media restrictions. State broadcasting dominant. Independent
media operating but censored on political content. Journalists occasionally
detained. Freedom House rated 'Partly Free'.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press, Kenya 1999

2000.assessment:
No RSF data for Kenya in 2000. Press freedom remained problematic
under late Moi. State media dominant but independent print media
relatively vibrant compared to neighbors. Some FM radio beginning.
Journalists faced legal harassment.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press, Kenya 2000

2001.assessment:
No RSF data for Kenya in 2001. Press freedom problematic but improving
slightly as Moi lame duck period began and transition became imminent.
Independent FM radio stations growing. Print media active.
Some pre-transition political opening for media.

sources.citation: Freedom House Freedom of the Press, Kenya 2001

2002.assessment:
No RSF data for Kenya in 2002. Election year brought improved media
environment. NARC coalition campaigned on media freedom. Competitive
election produced more open media coverage than previous elections.
RSF began ranking in 2002 — Kenya's inaugural rank was 71st.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index, Kenya 2002 (inaugural ranking)

2003.assessment:
Post-regime change, press freedom significantly improved under Kibaki.
Independent media expanded, new FM stations licensed, Kenya Broadcasting
Corporation partially liberalized. RSF ranked Kenya relatively well in early
post-KANU years. Freedom House upgraded to 'Free'.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2003, Kenya; Freedom House 2003

2004.assessment:
Press freedom moderately good in 2004. Independent media flourishing.
Some government interference in Anglo Leasing coverage. Journalists
faced occasional harassment. Overall much improved from Moi era.
Private FM stations and TV channels expanded.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2004, Kenya

2005.assessment:
Press freedom adequate in 2005. Constitution referendum coverage was
relatively balanced. Some government pressure on media over reform coverage.
Multiple private TV and radio stations operating. Print media vibrant.
Journalists occasionally harassed but media sector growing.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2005, Kenya; Freedom House Kenya 2005

2006.assessment:
Press freedom adequate in 2006. Kenya media landscape vibrant with
multiple competing outlets. Some political pressure and harassment of
journalists covering corruption. Overall one of Africa's better
environments for press freedom.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2006, Kenya

2007.assessment:
Press freedom declining in 2007. Election year media environment
showed government pressure on coverage. Post-election violence
(December) led to some media blackouts and journalist attacks.
RSF score declining as political pressure on media increased.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2007, Kenya

2008.assessment:
Press freedom significantly worsened in 2008 due to post-election violence.
Government banned live coverage of PEV (January 2008) via Communications
Commission order. Several journalists beaten. Media blackouts during peak
violence. Grand Coalition restored some normalcy by mid-2008. RSF ranked
Kenya much lower than prior years.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2008, Kenya; CPJ Kenya 2008

2009.assessment:
Press freedom partially recovering in 2009 after PEV restrictions lifted.
Media landscape resuming normal operations. Some legal threats to journalists
covering corruption or ICC cases. Adequate but below pre-2007 levels.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2009, Kenya

2010.assessment:
Press freedom improving in 2010. New constitution embedded media freedom
provisions. Media industry active. Some ICC-related pressures on journalists.
New Broadcasting Content Advisory Council forming. Problematic but improving.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2010, Kenya

2011.assessment:
Press freedom moderately problematic in 2011. Media landscape active
with many independent outlets. Some harassment of journalists covering
corruption and Somalia operations. Government increasingly sensitive
to negative coverage as 2013 elections approached.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2011, Kenya

2012.assessment:
Press freedom problematic in 2012. Media Bill (restricting press) debated.
Journalists covering ICC cases faced pressure. Pre-election environment
increasingly sensitive. Multiple vibrant outlets but with growing legal threats.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2012, Kenya; CPJ Kenya 2012

2013.assessment:
Press freedom deteriorated in 2013. Kenya Information and Communications
Act amendments gave government power to fine and suspend media outlets.
Kenyatta government sensitive to ICC coverage. Media owners under business
pressure from government. RSF ranked Kenya poorly.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2013, Kenya

2014.assessment:
Press freedom continued to deteriorate in 2014. Security Laws Amendment
Act introduced provisions restricting reporting on terrorism/security
matters. Media Council Act restricted journalist registration.
RSF ranked Kenya around 90th globally. Significant deterioration from post-2002 highs.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2014, Kenya

2015.assessment:
Press freedom remained problematic in 2015. Security Laws Amendment
in effect, restricting terror-related reporting. Media houses under
ownership pressure. Journalists self-censoring. RSF ranked Kenya around
100th globally. Vibrant media landscape but increasing legal restrictions.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2015, Kenya

2016.assessment:
Press freedom problematic in 2016. Media owners under government pressure.
Kenyatta government used advertising leverage against critical outlets.
Journalists faced harassment and legal action. RSF ranked Kenya poorly.
Multiple vibrant outlets but with structural constraints.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2016, Kenya

2017.assessment:
Press freedom significantly worsened in 2017. Election year saw government
signal displeasure with media. Three major TV stations (NTV, KTN, Citizen)
were shut down by government in January 2018 following October 2017
opposition parallel inauguration coverage (effect felt in late 2017).
RSF ranked Kenya ~96th. Election coverage under pressure.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2017, Kenya; CPJ Kenya 2017

2018.assessment:
Press freedom at low point in 2018. Three major TV stations shut down
for five days (February 2018) after broadcasting Odinga's 'swearing in'.
Government used Communications Authority to enforce shutdown. RSF ranked
Kenya poorly. This was one of Kenya's worst years for press freedom.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2018, Kenya; media shutdown Feb 2018

2019.assessment:
Press freedom slightly improving in 2019 after 2018 low. Handshake
between Kenyatta and Odinga reduced political temperature. TV stations
restored. Legal threats to journalists continuing but fewer shutdowns.
Media landscape active. RSF ranked Kenya ~100th.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2019, Kenya

2020.assessment:
Press freedom marginally improving in 2020. COVID-19 created new challenges
including misinformation laws used against journalists. Media landscape vibrant.
Some harassment of journalists covering government COVID response.
RSF ranked Kenya ~100th. Problematic but not as severe as 2017-2018.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2020, Kenya

2021.assessment:
Press freedom moderately problematic in 2021. Pre-election period
increasing government pressure on media. Online freedom laws contested.
Media landscape diverse but with legal uncertainties. RSF ranked Kenya ~101st.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2021, Kenya

2022.assessment:
Press freedom improving slightly in 2022. Peaceful election year saw
fairly free media coverage. Ruto government election platform included
media freedom commitments. RSF ranked Kenya ~95th. Problematic but improving.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2022, Kenya

2023.assessment:
Press freedom improving under Ruto. Some cases of journalist harassment
during Finance Bill protests (June 2023) but overall environment more
open. RSF ranked Kenya ~96th. Media actively covered Finance Bill protests.
Some journalists covering protests roughed up by police.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2023, Kenya

2024.assessment:
Press freedom remained problematic in 2024. Gen Z protests (June-July 2024)
led to journalists being targeted by police and protesters. Some internet
disruptions during protests. RSF ranked Kenya approximately 95-100th.
Media active and covering protests despite risks.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2024, Kenya

2025.assessment:
Press freedom expected to remain problematic in 2025 based on recent
trend. RSF 2025 rankings not yet available (as of March 2025). Kenya
media landscape vibrant but journalists face legal and physical risks.
Approximately 95-100th globally anticipated.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index trend 2020-2024, Kenya

1995.assessment:
No WB Statistical Capacity Indicator (SCI) data for Kenya in 1995.
Kenya National Bureau of Statistics (KNBS, then Central Bureau of Statistics)
was functional. Kenya had strong East African statistical tradition.
1989 census conducted; national accounts and trade statistics published.
Moderate statistical capacity estimated.

sources.citation: Kenya National Bureau of Statistics historical records

1996.assessment:
No WB SCI data for Kenya in 1996. Central Bureau of Statistics operational.
Annual Economic Survey and Statistical Abstract published. Trade and fiscal
data collected and published with some delays. Moderate statistical capacity.

sources.citation: Kenya Statistical Abstract 1996, Central Bureau of Statistics

1997.assessment:
No WB SCI data for Kenya in 1997. CBS continued statistical operations
despite governance challenges. Statistical capacity maintained at moderate
levels, above regional average for East Africa.

sources.citation: Kenya Statistical Abstract 1997

1998.assessment:
No WB SCI data for Kenya in 1998. Statistical operations continuing.
Population census preparations for 1999 underway. National accounts
system being updated. IMF DSBB subscription demonstrating improving
data dissemination standards.

sources.citation: Kenya Statistical Abstract 1998; IMF DSBB records

1999.assessment:
No WB SCI data for Kenya in 1999. Population and Housing Census conducted
in 1999, demonstrating significant statistical capacity. IMF DSBB subscriber.
Good to strong statistical capacity relative to Sub-Saharan Africa.

sources.citation: Kenya Population and Housing Census 1999

2000.assessment:
No WB SCI data for Kenya in 2000. Post-census data being processed and
published. PRSP data requirements improving statistical scope.
Moderate to good statistical capacity. Kenya Statistical Service
publishing regularly.

sources.citation: Kenya Statistical Abstract 2000; World Bank Kenya PRSP process

2001.assessment:
No WB SCI data for Kenya in 2001. CBS publications continuing. Statistical
capacity reasonable. IMF data standards adherence. Kenya Integrated
Household Budget Survey preparations underway. Moderate capacity.

sources.citation: Kenya Statistical Abstract 2001

2002.assessment:
No WB SCI data for Kenya in 2002. Regime change year. CBS operational.
Statistical capacity maintained during transition. New government (December 2002)
expected to strengthen statistical transparency. Moderate capacity.

sources.citation: Kenya Statistical Abstract 2002

2003.assessment:
No WB SCI data for Kenya in 2003. WB SCI began in 2004. Kibaki government
committed to strengthening statistical systems. Kenya Integrated Household
Budget Survey (KIHBS) launched 2003. Statistical capacity improving post-regime change.

sources.citation: Kenya KIHBS 2003; World Bank Kenya statistical capacity assessment

2004.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score was approximately 62 in 2004 (first available year).
Good statistical capacity relative to Sub-Saharan Africa. KNBS (rebranded
from CBS) publishing regular data. Kenya Integrated Household Budget Survey
ongoing. KIHBS and national accounts system improving.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2004

2005.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score was approximately 64 in 2005. Improving statistical
capacity under Kibaki. KIHBS data being processed. National accounts
rebasing ongoing. Kenya above Sub-Saharan African average.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2005

2006.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score was approximately 67 in 2006. Good statistical
capacity. KNBS publishing key indicators. National accounts chain-linked.
Statistical improvements part of Vision 2030 preparation.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2006

2007.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 67 in 2007. Stable good statistical
capacity. Post-election violence at year end had limited impact on
statistical operations. KNBS continued data publication.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2007

2008.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score fell slightly to approximately 64 in 2008.
Post-election violence disrupted some statistical fieldwork. Population
census postponed from 2008 to 2009. Statistical capacity somewhat affected
but KNBS remained operational.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2008

2009.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 64 in 2009. Population and Housing
Census conducted in August 2009. Strong statistical exercise demonstrating
good capacity. KNBS strengthened by new Statistics Act 2006.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2009; Kenya Census 2009

2010.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score improved to approximately 67 in 2010. Post-census
data being published and processed. New constitution embedded statistical
transparency requirements. KNBS publishing comprehensive economic data.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2010

2011.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 68 in 2011. KNBS continued strong
statistical output. Census results fully published. National accounts
rebased to 2001. Multiple surveys conducted. Above-average regional capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2011

2012.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 68 in 2012. Stable good statistical
capacity. KNBS publications regular. County devolution preparations
required new sub-national statistical frameworks.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2012

2013.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 67 in 2013. Devolution began; county
statistical offices being established. National capacity maintained.
Some complexity in sub-national data integration.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2013

2014.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 67 in 2014. Stable statistical capacity.
National accounts rebased to 2009 base year in 2014, significantly revising
GDP estimates upward. This revision improved data quality assessments.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2014; KNBS GDP rebasing 2014

2015.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 68 in 2015. Post-GDP rebasing improvements
in national accounts methodology. KNBS strengthened. Regular economic
surveys and censuses on schedule. Good capacity maintained.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2015

2016.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 67 in 2016. Stable statistical capacity.
KNBS continuing regular publications. Economic Survey, Statistical Abstract,
and sector reports available. IMF SDDS subscriber.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2016

2017.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 67 in 2017. Stable. Population and
Housing Census preparations for 2019 underway. Election year had limited
statistical impact. KNBS operational and publishing.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2017

2018.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 68 in 2018. Continued good statistical
capacity. Preparation for 2019 census advanced. Kenya Economic Survey
2018 published. KNBS providing regular statistical releases.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2018

2019.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 68 in 2019. Population and Housing
Census conducted in August 2019 demonstrating strong statistical capacity.
KNBS modern technology used for census enumeration. Above-average African capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2019; Kenya Census 2019

2020.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 65 in 2020. COVID-19 disrupted some
statistical fieldwork and surveys. KNBS adapted with some online/phone
surveys. Census data being processed and published. Good capacity maintained.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2020

2021.assessment:
Kenya's WB SCI score approximately 66 in 2021. WB SCI program was
discontinued after 2021, so this is the final data point. Good statistical
capacity maintained post-COVID. Census results being published in detail.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Kenya, 2021 (final year)

2022.assessment:
WB SCI discontinued after 2021. Kenya statistical capacity remained good
in 2022. KNBS continuing regular publications. National accounts, economic
survey, and sector statistics published. Good capacity estimated based on
institutional continuity.

sources.citation: KNBS Kenya Economic Survey 2022

2023.assessment:
WB SCI not available from 2022. Kenya's statistical capacity remains good.
KNBS published Economic Survey 2023, national accounts, and multiple sector
surveys. Good African statistical system. Statistical Advisory Committee active.

sources.citation: KNBS Kenya Economic Survey 2023

2024.assessment:
WB SCI not available from 2022. Kenya statistical capacity continues at
good level. KNBS operational. Regular statistical releases. Kenya among
better African statistical systems, above regional average.

sources.citation: KNBS Kenya Economic Survey 2024

2025.assessment:
WB SCI discontinued. Kenya statistical capacity remains good in 2025.
KNBS is one of Africa's stronger national statistics offices. Regular
publications and surveys maintained. Good relative to Sub-Saharan Africa.

sources.citation: KNBS Kenya overview 2025
